payload.text: احذف النظام الذكاء الاصطناعي

**Reframing the Architecture**

I've been examining the file structure and pinpointed the main areas for the "AI" element removal. The `Header.tsx` file clearly contains a navigation component linked to the `STUDIO` mode, while `App.tsx` actively handles it. Furthermore, `QuestionBuilder.tsx` seems to be the central hub for the AI integration with the Gemini API. These locations will need modification.


**Removing AI Elements**

I've made progress on removing the AI elements. I've successfully taken out the `AppMode.STUDIO` enum, the Studio button from the header, and the `QuestionBuilder` import. I'm now cleaning up the `App.tsx` file, removing the `editingId` state and associated logic. I'm also removing references to the global sync HUD as it's no longer relevant. My focus is now on removing the remaining AI-related state and visual cues.


**Clarifying the AI Scope**

I've determined the primary AI component is `QuestionBuilder.tsx`.  I'm removing the Studio mode, the header button, and simplifying `App.tsx`. I'll remove `editingId` and associated logic, and also take out the "Global Sync HUD," as its language might imply AI. I'm focusing on removing all AI-specific state and UI elements, and confirming that the "Search" and "Speech" functionalities are not AI-powered, but native browser features.


**Refining the Removal**

I'm now in the process of tidying up and making further edits to the code base. I'm focusing on ensuring all AI-related elements are gone. My current task is removing AI references from `types.ts`, `components/Header.tsx`, and `App.tsx`, focusing on thoroughness. I have also verified that `metadata.json` doesn't need modification, so I'm progressing with confidence.

entries.description: Remove STUDIO mode from AppMode enum.
Remove the Studio navigation button from the header.
Remove AI Studio integration, question builder, and cosmetic sync HUD.

payload.text: Add a flashcard mode to the quiz component.

**Developing New Flashcard View**

I'm currently fleshing out the design for a "Flashcard mode" within the `Quiz` component. This new mode will exist alongside the existing views like `setup`, `playing`, etc. The existing modes (`exam`, `practice`, `mistakes`) need to be considered in relation to this new feature, and how the flashcard interface will integrate. The goal is to provide a seamless user experience that's both intuitive and effective for studying.


**Adding Flashcard Mode**

I've made great strides in integrating the flashcard mode. I've added 'flashcard' as a mode to `QuizSession` and made relevant updates to the `setup` view in `Quiz.tsx` to enable selecting this mode. Now, I'm working on the `playing` view logic, ensuring it displays a flip-able card instead of multiple-choice options when the flashcard mode is active. I plan to use the core flip functionality. I'm also adding states to the quiz session to allow users to mark flashcards as correct or incorrect, driving progress as they go.


**Finalizing Flashcard Features**

I'm nearing completion of the flashcard features. I've integrated buttons for "Got it" and "Missed it" within the flashcard view to allow for user self-reporting of answers. I updated `QuizSettings` and `QuizSession` in `types.ts` to accommodate the `flashcard` mode. The setup UI has been enhanced to select flashcard mode, and the `playing` state now incorporates the flip functionality with the score tracking. The system has reached a stable state.

entries.description: Update QuizSettings and QuizSession interfaces to include flashcard mode.
Implement flashcard mode within the Quiz component, allowing users to self-assess their knowledge in a structured session.

payload.text: Ensure the Flashcards component is fully accessible by adding appropriate ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation support (e.g., using arrow keys for next/previous card), and ensuring sufficient color contrast.

**Enhancing Accessibility Focus**

I'm currently focused on the ARIA attributes. I've been researching the most appropriate roles and labels for the buttons, the card, and the filter menu. The goal is to make the `Flashcards` component fully accessible to screen readers, allowing users to navigate and understand the interactive elements effectively. I am making rapid progress on this task.


**Improving Keyboard Interactions**

I've been concentrating on integrating keyboard navigation. The `Flashcards` component is getting keyboard controls with arrow keys to navigate and space/enter to flip cards. I'm adding `aria-live` to the card container for screen reader announcements. Also, focus states are being refined to guide keyboard users, and I'm ensuring the 3D flip animation functions correctly with keyboard triggers.


**Refining Accessibility Details**

I'm now incorporating `aria-haspopup` and `aria-expanded` attributes into the filter dropdown button. This should help screen reader users understand the interactive nature of this element. Simultaneously, I'm adding descriptive labels to the navigation buttons. The goal is to make the entire interface even more intuitive and navigable for all users.

entries.description: Enhanced accessibility: added keyboard navigation (arrows to navigate, space/enter to flip), ARIA attributes for screen readers, and improved focus management.

A) Rheumatics
B) Podiatry
C) Orthopedics
D) Cardiology
E) Dermatology

2. How many named bones are found in the adult human skeleton?
A) 200
B) 206
C) 212
D) 227
E) 250
B
3. Which of the following statements is NOT true?
A) The axial skeleton has 80 bones.
B) The appendicular skeleton has 126 bones.
C) The axial skeleton is composed of the bones that run through the axis of the body.
D) Children have more bones than adults.
E) The appendicular skeleton does not include the pelvic and pectoral girdles.
E

B) Ribs
C) Vertebrae
D) Carpals

D) They help form joints.

C) fossa.
D) meatus.
E) sulcus.

B) condyle.
C) tuberosity.
D) head.
E) trochanter.
D
13. A prominent ridge or elongated projection on a bone is called a
A) crest.
B) trochanter.
C) sulcus.
D) fossa.
E) tubercle.
A
14. The tube-like passageway found in the temporal bone that allows sound waves to reach the eardrum is an example of which of the following bone surface markings?
A) fissure.
B) foramen.
C) fossa.
D) meatus.
E) sulcus.
D

payload.text:
ضيف هي الاسئلة chapter 9
Terms in this set (67)
Which of the following types of joints lacks a joint cavity and is held together by a fibrous connective tissue?
1. Fibrous joints
2. Cartilaginous joints
3. Synovial joints

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 1 and 2
e) All of these choices
A- 1 only
Which of the following types of joints do NOT have a synovial cavity?
1. Fibrous joints
2. Cartilaginous joints
3. Synovial joints

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 1 and 2
e) All of these choices
D- 1 & 2
Which functional class of joints contains joints that are freely movable?
1. Synarthrosis
2. Amphiarthrosis
3. Diarthrosis

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) Both 2 and 3
e) All of these choices
C- 3 only
Which of the following is a type of fibrous joint composed of a thin layer of dense irregular fibrous connective tissue found between the bones of the skull?
1. Syndesmoses
2. Gomphosis
3. Suture

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) Both 1 and 2
e) None of these choices
C- 3 only
A suture joint fits into which functional joint classification?

a) synarthrosis.
b) amphiarthrosis.
c) diarthrosis.
d) synovial.
e) cartilaginous.
A
The epiphyseal plate in a long bone is an example of which type of joint?

a) Gomphosis
b) Suture
c) Symphysis
d) Synovial
e) Synchondrosis
E- Synchondrosis
The joint between the first rib and the manubrium of the sternum is classified as

a) a synchondrosis.
b) a synarthrosis.
c) a cartilaginous joint.
d) All of these choices.
e) None of these choices.
D- All of these choices
Which of the following is NOT a common characteristic of synovial joints?

a) Contain a joint cavity
b) Are freely moveable
c) Articulating bones are covered with hyaline cartilage
d) Include elastic cartilage
e) Have ligaments holding the joint together
D- Include elastic cartilage
Which of the following is(are) made from dense regular connective tissue?

a) Ligaments
b) Articular cartilage
c) Articular fat pads
d) Synovial membrane
e) Synovial fluid
A- Ligaments
Which of the following structures include the fibular and tibial collateral ligaments of the knee joint?

a) Synovial membranes

payload.text: زبط القياسات حتى يزبط مع التلفون

**Examining Mobile Responsiveness**

I'm currently focused on tackling the mobile responsiveness problems. I've begun to evaluate the key components, and have observed they are structured as `App.tsx`, `components/Header.tsx`, `components/Flashcards.tsx`, and `components/Quiz.tsx`. My current focus is to identify the elements causing the layout issues.



**Investigating CSS Adjustments**

I'm now diving into the CSS files, specifically `components/Flashcards.tsx` and `components/Quiz.tsx`. My current task is to review the Tailwind classes to pinpoint any mobile viewport issues. For `Flashcards.tsx`, I'm looking at potential problems with `h-[calc(100vh-6rem)]`, font sizes, and the bottom controls height. In `Quiz.tsx`, the focus is on the mobile navigation logic and the layout changes for different screen sizes.


**Reviewing Quiz and QuestionList**

I'm now focusing on `components/QuestionList.tsx` and `components/Header.tsx`, making sure they look good on a variety of devices. The image in the list items of `QuestionList` might take up too much vertical space. I'm also confirming that the header is responsive and the navigation is streamlined. I'm checking `components/Quiz.tsx` to look at how the question text, zoom modal and setup screen behave on mobile.


**Prioritizing Mobile Optimization**

I've been examining the quirks of `h-screen` and recognizing the potential of `dvh` for better viewport height control, along with ensuring `overflow-hidden` is set on the body. I'm prioritizing improvements for mobile on Flashcards, specifically looking at the card height overflow problems and refining the button sizes and responsiveness of images. I'm also addressing the Quiz's navigation drawer behavior and fine-tuning font sizes to improve content visibility without excessive scrolling. I am considering changes to the `index.html` file's `bg-slate-50` value as well, for performance.


**Focusing on Mobile Refinements**

I'm now zeroing in on specific mobile refinements. Starting with `App.tsx`, I'll implement `h-[100dvh]` to address the viewport height inconsistencies. Next, in `Flashcards.tsx`, I plan to adjust container height logic and fine-tune padding and image max-height for better mobile rendering. I will make the card content scrollable if it exceeds the height. In Quiz, I'll ensure both the question text and options fit well and optimize the mobile navigation toggle. Finally, I will ensure `Header.tsx` doesn't shrink on small screens.


**Implementing Mobile Layout**

I'm now implementing the planned modifications. I've switched `h-screen` to `h-[100dvh]` in `App.tsx` and started adjusting padding in `Flashcards.tsx` to `px-3` on small screens, refining the layout, and constraining image sizes. In `Quiz.tsx`, I'm looking at adjusting padding and font sizes as well, while in `QuestionList.tsx`, I am working on creating a more compact view for mobile screens. The goal is to ensure a better fit for phone screens, while maintaining readability and usability.


**Improving Mobile Rendering**

I'm now focusing on refining the mobile layout for various components. In `Quiz.tsx`, I'll adjust the text sizes and ensure the mobile drawer functions smoothly. For `Flashcards.tsx`, I'll remove the explicit height calculation, allowing it to fill the available space and removing conflicts with `App.tsx`. I'll also change the logo text size in `Header.tsx` for a better fit, and hide or downsize the "Knowledge Index" banner in `QuestionList.tsx` on small screens to reduce vertical space. My main focus is a consistent and user-friendly mobile experience.

To ensure the application is fully responsive and optimized for mobile devices ("fixing measurements for phone"), I will update the main layout structure to use dynamic viewport units (`dvh`) which handle mobile browser address bars better. I will also adjust padding, font sizes, and layout flex properties in the Header, Flashcards, and Quiz components to maximize screen real estate on smaller devices.

Here are the changes:

1.  **App.tsx**: Switch to `h-[100dvh]` to prevent scrolling issues caused by mobile browser toolbars.
2.  **components/Header.tsx**: Compact the logo and navigation for mobile screens.
3.  **components/Flashcards.tsx**: Optimize the card container height and image sizing for vertical mobile screens.
4.  **components/Quiz.tsx**: Adjust padding and font sizes to make the quiz experience smoother on touch devices.
5.  **components/QuestionList.tsx**: Make the list view more compact on mobile.

entries.description: Use dynamic viewport height (dvh) for better mobile browser support and ensure main content area fills available space correctly.
Make header more compact on mobile to save vertical space.
Optimize flashcards for mobile viewport height and touch interactions.
Adjust quiz layout for mobile: better font sizes, padding, and navigation drawer.
Make list view more compact on mobile.

b) joint
Most muscles cross at least one
a) tendon
b) joint
c) bone
d) ligament
e) body plane
c) origin, insertion
The attachment of a muscle's tendon to the stationary bone is called the _____; the attachment of the muscle's other tendon to the movable bone is called the _____.
a) origin, action
b) insertion, action
c) origin, insertion
d) insertion, origin
e) insertion, action
d) effort and load.
A lever is acted on at two different points by two different forces which are called the
a) fulcrum and resistance.
b) leverage and load.
c) lever and resistance.
d) effort and load.
e) lever and effort.
a) effort, load
Motion will occur in a muscle when the _____ supplied exceeds the _____.
a) effort, load
b) resistance, lever
c) load, effort
d) load, resistance
e) lever, effort
A lever operates at a mechanical advantage when a smaller effort can move a heavier load. The effort moves a greater distance at a faster rate than the load. A lever operates at a mechanical disadvantage when a larger effort moves a lighter load. The effort moves more slowly and for a shorter distance than the load. When the load is far from the fulcrum and the effort is applied near the fulcrum, the effort applied must be greater than the load to be moved. This type of leverage is mechanically advantageous because it allows the load to be moved rapidly through a large distance, with only minimal muscle shortening.
With reference to the effort and load, describe the main difference between a lever operating with a mechanical advantage versus a lever operating at a mechanical disadvantage.
If the fulcrum is between the effort and the load it is a first class lever. Scissors are an example of a first class lever. A first class lever can produce either a mechanical advantage or disadvantage depending on whether the effort or load is closer to the fulcrum. If the load is between the fulcrum and the effort, it is a second class lever. A wheel barrow is an example of a second class lever. They operate at a mechanical advantage because the load is always closer to the fulcrum than the effort. If the effort is between the fulcrum and the load, it is a third class lever. Forceps are an example of a third class lever. They operate at a mechanical disadvantage because the effort is always closer to the fulcrum than the load.
Briefly describe the three categories of levers based on the position of their fulcrum. Give an example of each type of lever and state whether the lever works at a mechanical advantage, disadvantage or both
c) oval
Which of the following is NOT a common arrangement of muscle fascicles?
a) pennate
b) triangular
c) oval
d) parallel
e) fusiform
a) triangular
This type of fascicle arrangement has the fascicles spread over a broad area and converges at a thick central tendon.
a) triangular
b) pennate
c) circular
d) fusiform
e) multipennate
b) agonist.
The muscle that serves as the "prime mover" during a movement is called the
a) antagonist.
b) agonist.
c) synergist.
d) asynergist.
e) fixator.
d) fixator
This type of muscle works by stabilizing the origin of the agonist so that it can act more efficiently.
a) synergist
b) agonist
c) antagonist
d) fixator
e) secondary mover
Stretching can improve physical performance by increasing joint flexibility allowing the joint to move through a greater range of motion. Stretching decreases risk of injury by decreasing resistance in various soft tissues so there is less of a chance of exceeding maximum tissue extensibility during activity. Stretching can reduce some of the muscle soreness that results after exercise. Stretching can help realign soft tissues to improve and maintain good posture.
Briefly describe four main benefits of stretching before exercising.
e) Thickness of fibers
Which of the following is NOT a characteristic commonly used to name skeletal muscles?
a) Size
b) Shape
c) Number of origins
d) Sites of origins
e) Thickness of fibers
The bone is the lever. The joint is the fulcrum. The muscle contraction pulling on its insertion point is the effort. The weight of the part to be moved is the resistance. A first class lever has the fulcrum between the effort and the resistance; a second class lever has the resistance in the middle; the third class lever has the effort in the middle. In all cases, the lever moves around the fulcrum.
What anatomical structures of the musculoskeletal system correspond to the components of a lever system? Describe the arrangement of these parts in first, second, and third class lever systems.
The agonist (prime mover) contracts to cause a particular movement. The antagonist causes the opposite action, and so, must relax while the agonist contracts. Synergists prevent unwanted movements during an action or aid the agonist during the movement, while fixators stabilize the origin of the agonist. Both allow the agonist to work more efficiently.
Discuss the roles of agonists, antagonists, synergists, and fixators in movement.
The tendon of the scapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor make up the rotator cuff. The most commonly injured structure of the rotator cuff is the tendon of the supraspinatus muscle. Any activity involving vigorous circumduction of shoulder can result in a rotor cuff injury. Common examples of activities that lead to this injury include throwing baseballs, playing racket sports, and swimming
Chuck has a rotator cuff injury. Which muscles and associated structures are most commonly involved in this type of injury? What sorts of activities commonly cause this injury?
1. Thenar: four muscles that act on the thumb and form the lateral rounded contour of the palm. 2. Hypothenar: three muscles that act on the little finger and form the medial rounded contour of the palm. 3. Intermediate: 12 muscles acting on all digits except the thumb; subgrouped as lumbricals, palmar interossei, and dorsal interossei, and located between metacarpals; needed for all phalangeal movements
Name the three groups of muscles constituting the intrinsic muscles of the hand. Briefly describe the structure and function of each of these groups.
The diaphragm forms the floor of the thoracic cavity. It flattens (contracts) during inspiration to increase the size of the thoracic cavity. The external intercostals are found between the ribs and they act to increase the lateral and anteroposterior dimensions of the thorax. Internal intercostals are also found between ribs and they act by pulling the ribs together to decrease the size of the thoracic cavity. The diaphragm relaxes during expiration to form a dome that decreases the size of the thoracic cavity. The anterior scalenes and the pectoralis minor also assist during forced inspirations.
Name and describe the locations and actions of the muscles typically used in breathing
